data.title: Stanford University to Sell $327 Million of Taxable Bonds
data.description:
By Chris Wack

Stanford University is offering $327 million of taxable bonds, and plans to use proceeds from the sale for general purposes at the university.

The university is selling Series 2025 A taxable bonds, which will be due March 1, 2035, according to documents posted Thursday on MuniOS.

Interest payments will be made semiannually on March 1 and Sept. 1, with the first payment due Sept. 1, 2025.

The bonds are unsecured general obligations of the university, and aren't secured by a reserve fund.

Stanford is a research and teaching university, with both graduate and undergraduate programs. For the fall 2024 semester, the university had 7,550 undergraduate and 9,900 graduate students. Its main campus consists of 8,400 acres of land near Palo Alto, Calif.

Sources of revenue include student tuition and fees, and revenue from healthcare services, sponsored support and investment income, according to the preliminary official statement.

Moody's rated the securities Aaa, and S&P Global Ratings and Fitch Ratings have them rated at AAA.

Morgan Stanley is the lead manager on the deal.

Write to Chris Wack at chris.wack@wsj.com

data.title: Primerica, Inc. SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Primerica, Inc., a leading provider of financial products to middle-income households in the U.S. and Canada, has released its 2024 10-K report. The report details the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces in the current market environment.

Financial Highlights

Primerica, Inc. reported strong financial performance for the year 2024, with several key metrics showing significant growth:

  • Total Revenues: $3,089 million, a 12% increase from 2023, driven by higher commissions and fees, net premiums, and net investment income.
  • Net Income: $471 million, an 18% decrease from 2023, primarily due to a loss from discontinued operations.
  • Income from Continuing Operations: $720 million, a 22% increase from 2023, reflecting strong performance in the Term Life Insurance and Investment and Savings Products segments.
  • Income from Continuing Operations Before Income Taxes: $939 million, a 22% increase from 2023, driven by higher revenues and controlled expenses.
  • Net Income Attributable to Primerica, Inc.: $471 million, reflecting a decrease due to discontinued operations, but strong underlying business performance.

Business Highlights

Primerica's business model and operational performance in 2024 showcased significant growth and strategic advancements:

  • Business Model: Primerica operates through a large network of over 151,000 life insurance-licensed independent sales representatives, focusing on middle-income households in the U.S. and Canada. This model emphasizes face-to-face interactions and entrepreneurial opportunities for representatives.
  • Sales Force Growth: The number of life insurance-licensed independent sales representatives increased to 151,611 by the end of 2024, up from 141,572 in 2023.
  • Product Portfolio: Primerica offers a range of financial products including term life insurance, mutual funds, annuities, and managed investments. The company focuses on term life insurance as a primary offering, with over 3 million policies in force by the end of 2024.
  • New Product Launches: In October 2022, Primerica introduced new term life insurance products, PowerTerm and PrecisionTerm, which offer rapid issue and traditional underwriting options, respectively.
  • Sales Units: In 2024, Primerica issued 370,396 new life insurance policies, an increase from 358,860 in 2023, reflecting the company's growth in policy issuance.
  • Geographical Performance: The company conducts its core business activities in the U.S. through entities like Primerica Life Insurance Company and in Canada through Primerica Life Insurance Company of Canada, with a strong presence in both regions.
  • Digital Capabilities: Primerica is enhancing its digital capabilities, including the Primerica App and TurboApps, to streamline operations and improve client and representative experiences.
  • Future Outlook: Looking forward to 2025 and beyond, Primerica plans to expand its digital experiences, deepen its talent pool, and ensure its corporate image reflects its values and mission.
  • Training and Support: The company provides extensive training and support tools through Primerica Online (POL) and the Primerica App, which are crucial for the development and success of its independent sales force.
  • Operational Scalability: Primerica's distribution model and support systems are designed to be highly scalable, allowing for efficient growth without proportionate increases in overhead expenses.

Strategic Initiatives

Primerica has undertaken several strategic initiatives to ensure long-term growth and financial stability:

  • Investment Strategy: The company maintains a conservative investment strategy to preserve invested assets and ensure liquidity for prompt payment of claims. This includes managing the portfolio's composition with restrictions on asset type, credit quality, and duration to mitigate risks.
  • Capital Management: Primerica declared and paid ordinary dividends of $311.8 million from its life insurance subsidiaries and $208.4 million from non-life insurance subsidiaries to the Parent Company. The Parent Company had cash and invested assets of $496.8 million at year-end. The company also manages its capital structure through senior unsecured notes, a redundant reserve financing transaction, and a revolving credit facility.
  • Future Outlook: The company plans to continue its conservative investment strategy and maintain sufficient liquidity to meet operating requirements over the next 12 months. It will also explore potential sources of funding to support long-term liquidity needs, including possible changes in share repurchases and borrowings.

Challenges and Risks

Primerica faces several challenges and risks that could impact its business operations and financial performance:

  • Distribution Structure: The company faces significant risks related to its distribution structure, including high turnover among independent sales representatives and challenges in recruiting and retaining new recruits. Changes in licensing requirements and increased regulatory scrutiny could also impact the size of the sales force.
  • Regulatory Risks: Regulatory risks are heightened by potential changes in laws governing independent contractor classifications, which could lead to increased costs and operational restructuring. The company also faces risks from potential violations of laws and regulations by independent sales representatives, which could result in material liabilities.
  • Insurance Business Risks: The company is exposed to risks from changes in mortality, persistency, and reinsurance markets. Regulatory changes in the insurance sector could impose more restrictive requirements, impacting profitability and operations. The company also faces risks from potential downgrades in financial strength ratings, which could affect its competitive position and ability to write new business.
  • Investment and Savings Products: The investment and savings products segment is heavily dependent on a limited platform of mutual fund and annuity products. Changes in consumer demand or relationships with product manufacturers could materially impact the business. Regulatory changes imposing heightened standards of conduct or restrictions on compensation could also adversely affect operations.
  • Economic and Geopolitical Risks: Management has identified economic downcycles and geopolitical events as significant risks that could impact consumer spending and investment decisions. Elevated inflation and interest rates could affect demand for the company's products and the recruitment of new sales representatives. The company is also exposed to risks from major public health crises or catastrophic events, which could disrupt operations and financial markets.
  • Market Risks: The company is exposed to market risks from interest rate fluctuations and credit deterioration in its invested asset portfolio. Changes in accounting standards and regulatory requirements could also impact financial reporting and statutory capital requirements. Currency fluctuations between the U.S. dollar and the Canadian dollar present additional financial risks.

SEC Filing: Primerica, Inc. [ PRI ] - 10-K - Feb. 28, 2025
